name: cpp
description: Modern C++ conventions (C++20/23), Core Guidelines, RAII, smart pointers, testing, and tooling for production code

decisions.decision: Target C++20 as baseline; adopt C++23 features via feature-test macros; do not use C++20 modules yet
decisions.rationale: C++20 has mature compiler support across GCC, Clang, and MSVC. C++23 features like std::expected and std::print are valuable but not universally available. C++20 modules lack ecosystem maturity (major libraries have not adopted them, cross-compiler interop is incomplete).
decisions.details:
Set CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD 20 with CMAKE_CXX_STANDARD_REQUIRED ON and CMAKE_CXX_EXTENSIONS OFF. Use C++23 features (std::expected, std::print) via __cpp_lib_* feature-test macros or compiler version checks.

C++20 features to adopt: concepts, ranges, std::format, std::jthread, three-way comparison (<=>), designated initializers, constexpr improvements.

Do NOT use C++20 modules — stick with traditional headers. Revisit when ecosystem support matures.

decisions.decision: Use std::expected for recoverable errors; exceptions only for truly unrecoverable conditions
decisions.rationale: std::expected (C++23) provides type-safe error handling without hidden control flow or exception overhead. It encodes success and failure in the type system and supports monadic chaining (and_then, transform, or_else). Exceptions remain appropriate for programming errors and conditions that cannot be handled locally.
decisions.details:
Use std::expected<T, E> for all new API boundaries returning recoverable errors. Define a project-wide error enum or class for E.

Never call .value() without checking has_value() first. Use monadic chaining for pipelines: result.and_then(step2).transform(step3).or_else(handle_error).

Keep exceptions for: constructor failures, truly unrecoverable errors, and error handling spanning multiple call stack levels.

If C++23 is unavailable, use tl::expected (header-only polyfill) or std::optional for simpler cases.

decisions.decision: Ban raw new/delete; use smart pointers, containers, span, and string_view; enforce via Core Guidelines clang-tidy checks
decisions.rationale: Memory safety is under regulatory scrutiny (CISA/NSA guidance). Modern C++ can be made memory-safe through disciplined use of RAII, containers, and views. The C++ Core Guidelines codify these practices with tooling enforcement via clang-tidy.
decisions.details:
Ownership hierarchy (prefer in order):
1. Stack allocation (default)
2. std::unique_ptr (single ownership, zero overhead)
3. std::shared_ptr (shared ownership, reference-counted)
4. std::weak_ptr (non-owning observer, breaks cycles)
5. Raw pointer/reference (non-owning, observing only)

Use std::make_unique and std::make_shared (exception safety, single allocation for shared_ptr). Never pass smart pointers to express non-ownership — use T& or const T& instead.

Use std::span for buffer parameters (replaces pointer+size). Use std::string_view for read-only string parameters (replaces const char*). Use std::variant instead of unions. Use range-for instead of index-based iteration.

Enforce: enable cppcoreguidelines-* checks in clang-tidy.

decisions.decision: Follow the C++ Core Guidelines as the primary reference; enforce via clang-tidy cppcoreguidelines-* checks
decisions.rationale: The Core Guidelines (Stroustrup/Sutter) are a living document providing practical rules for safe, efficient C++. They are enforced by clang-tidy and supported by the Guidelines Support Library (GSL). Following them systematically addresses the most common C++ pitfalls.
decisions.details:
Enable cppcoreguidelines-* checks in clang-tidy. Consider Microsoft GSL for gsl::not_null<T> (prevents null pointer bugs) and gsl::narrow<T> (checked narrowing conversions).

Key principles: subset of superset (use safe extensions that make error-prone features redundant), static type safety, resource safety via RAII, zero-overhead abstractions.

decisions.decision: Use clang-tidy with broad checks enabled minus vendor-specific; cppcheck as complement
decisions.rationale: clang-tidy integrates with the compilation database for accurate analysis. Starting with all checks enabled and selectively disabling vendor-specific ones gives maximum coverage. cppcheck catches different classes of bugs with a low false-positive rate.
decisions.details:
Start with Checks: * then disable vendor-specific: -abseil-*, -altera-*, -android-*, -fuchsia-*, -google-*, -llvm*, -zircon-*.

Critical categories: bugprone-*, cert-*, cppcoreguidelines-*, clang-analyzer-*, modernize-*, performance-*, readability-*.

Integrate via CMAKE_CXX_CLANG_TIDY in CMake. Run in CI as a gate. Also run cppcheck as a complementary tool.

Place .clang-tidy in the project root.

decisions.decision: Use Catch2 v3 as primary test framework; CTest as runner; pair with Trompeloeil for mocking
decisions.rationale: Catch2 provides expressive BDD-style tests with low setup overhead. CTest integrates naturally with CMake. For enterprise codebases with existing infrastructure, Google Test with GoogleMock is equally valid.
decisions.details:
Catch2 v3: library (not header-only like v2), CMake integration via FetchContent or vcpkg. Supports BDD-style (GIVEN/WHEN/THEN), generators, matchers, and benchmarks.

Google Test: comprehensive, built-in mocking via GoogleMock, excellent IDE integration. Choose for large teams with existing GTest infrastructure.

For mocking with Catch2, use Trompeloeil or FakeIt. For property-based testing, use RapidCheck.

Organise: one test file per source file in tests/. Run via CTest in CI.

decisions.decision: Three sanitizer CI jobs: ASan+UBSan, TSan, and optionally MSan; define CMake presets for each
decisions.rationale: Sanitizers catch memory safety and concurrency bugs that tests alone miss. ASan, TSan, and MSan are mutually exclusive — they must run as separate CI jobs. CMake presets make sanitizer configurations reusable.
decisions.details:
Job 1: -fsanitize=address,undefined -fno-omit-frame-pointer (ASan + UBSan)
Job 2: -fsanitize=thread (TSan, for multithreaded code)
Job 3: -fsanitize=memory (MSan, Clang-only, optional)

Define CMake presets (CMakePresets.json) for each sanitizer configuration. Rebuild ALL dependencies with the same sanitizer flags.

Enable sanitizers in Debug builds. Set ASAN_OPTIONS and UBSAN_OPTIONS for better diagnostics.

decisions.decision: CMake 3.25+ with target-based configuration, CMakePresets.json, and Ninja as default generator
decisions.rationale: CMake is the de facto standard for C++ builds. Target-based commands (target_*) replace error-prone directory-scoped settings. CMakePresets provide reproducible, shareable build configurations. Ninja is significantly faster than Make for incremental builds.
decisions.details:
Core principles: target-centric (target_link_libraries, target_include_directories), PRIVATE/PUBLIC/INTERFACE for dependency visibility, generator expressions for portable targets, no global state (avoid set(CMAKE_CXX_FLAGS ...)).

CMakePresets.json: define hidden base presets for common settings, derive platform/compiler-specific presets via inherits. Include configure, build, and test preset types.

Dependencies: find_package for system libraries, FetchContent for small deps, vcpkg for larger dependency sets.

decisions.decision: Use vcpkg in manifest mode for dependency management; Conan 2 for complex cross-platform or embedded projects
decisions.rationale: vcpkg provides the tightest CMake integration, simple manifest mode (vcpkg.json), and prebuilt binary caching on GitHub Actions. Conan 2 is better for complex dependency trees, cross-compilation profiles, and private package hosting.
decisions.details:
vcpkg: place vcpkg.json at project root. Wire via CMakePresets.json toolchain file. Dependencies are built from source ensuring ABI compatibility. Binary caching speeds up CI.

Conan 2: use for large projects with many dependencies, embedded targets, or internal package ecosystems. Lockfiles for reproducibility. Profiles for cross-compilation.

For small projects or header-only deps, CMake FetchContent is sufficient.

decisions.decision: Use clang-format with a project-specific .clang-format config; enforce in CI
decisions.rationale: clang-format is the universal standard for C++ formatting. Committed config eliminates formatting debates. CI enforcement prevents style drift.
decisions.details:
Place .clang-format at project root. Start from BasedOnStyle: LLVM (or Google, Chromium). Customize minimally.

CI: clang-format --dry-run --Werror (non-zero exit on diff). Include in pre-commit hooks.

Use // clang-format off / // clang-format on for exceptional blocks.

decisions.decision: Adopt concepts, ranges, std::format, std::jthread, and three-way comparison in all new code
decisions.rationale: These C++20 features are production-ready with mature compiler support. Concepts replace SFINAE with clean constraint syntax. Ranges provide composable data pipelines. jthread auto-joins with cooperative cancellation. All reduce boilerplate and improve safety.
decisions.details:
Concepts: use for all template constraints (replaces enable_if/SFINAE).
Ranges: use std::ranges:: algorithms and views for data pipelines. C++23 ranges::to() materializes views.
std::format / std::print: type-safe formatting with compile-time checks. Use {fmt} as polyfill if needed.
std::jthread: RAII thread that auto-joins in destructor. Always prefer over std::thread.
Three-way comparison (<=>): reduces comparison operator boilerplate.
Designated initializers: Point{.x = 1, .y = 2} for readable aggregate init.

decisions.decision: Public headers in include/project_name/, private sources in src/, tests in tests/; CMakePresets.json for build configuration
decisions.rationale: Namespaced include paths prevent header collisions during installation. Separating public and private headers enforces API boundaries. CMakePresets.json provides reproducible builds across developer machines and CI.
decisions.details:
Layout:
  CMakeLists.txt
  CMakePresets.json (version-controlled)
  CMakeUserPresets.json (developer-local, gitignored)
  vcpkg.json
  .clang-format, .clang-tidy
  include/project_name/ — public headers
  src/ — implementation (.cpp + private headers)
  tests/ — test sources
  cmake/ — custom CMake modules
  docs/
